perturbation.original_text: DIVIDENDS. Each year, we will determine our divisible surplus. This contract's share, if any, will be credited as a dividend. Since we do not expect this contract to contribute to divisible surplus, it is not expected that any dividends will be credited.
perturbation.changed_text: DIVIDENDS. Each year, we will determine our divisible surplus. This contract's share, if any, will be credited as a dividend.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the sentence 'Since we do not expect this contract to contribute to divisible surplus, it is not expected that any dividends will be credited,' the contract now implies that dividends *might* be credited, contradicting later sections or expectations that assumed no dividends. This creates uncertainty about the contract's financial benefits and creates an in-text contradiction because the text is now missing information about the intention of paying dividends.
perturbation.contradicted_text: DIVIDEND OPTIONS. If dividends are credited after premiums can no longer be paid under this contract, dividends will be paid in cash. Otherwise, dividends will be applied under the Payment of Premium option unless the Cash option has been chosen in writing. Cash. Dividends are paid in cash. Payment of Premium. Dividends are applied as payment of a Net Premium.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that dividends were not expected to be credited. The changed text removes this statement, implying that dividends *might* be credited. This creates a direct contradiction with the 'DIVIDEND OPTIONS' section, which outlines how dividends will be handled if they *are* credited. Because the new text does not express they will not be credited, this directly impacts the dividend options section, creating uncertainty and unenforceability. Thus, there is a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ENTIRE CONTRACT In the ENTIRE CONTRACT section, the first phrase and numbered list are amended to read: The Entire Contract consists of: 1) This contract including any attached riders, amendments, or endorsements; 2) The Application attached to this contract; and 3) The Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws of the Society and all amendments to them. Benefits will not be reduced or eliminated by any future amendments to our Articles of Incorporation or Bylaws.
perturbation.changed_text: ENTIRE CONTRACT In the ENTIRE CONTRACT section, the first phrase and numbered list are amended to read: The Entire Contract consists of: 1) This contract including any attached riders, amendments, or endorsements; 2) The Application attached to this contract; and 3) The Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws of the Society and all amendments to them.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'Benefits will not be reduced or eliminated by any future amendments to our Articles of Incorporation or Bylaws.', the contract now implies that future amendments to the Articles of Incorporation or Bylaws *could* reduce or eliminate benefits, creating uncertainty about the long-term security of the contract. This omission contradicts the implied guarantee of stable benefits and creates an in-text contradiction because a key protection is removed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you may be required to make an extra payment. The Board of Directors will determine the amount of any extra payment. It will be based on each member's fair share of the deficiency. You may make the extra payment by an equivalent reduction in benefits or by a payment in cash. If you do not make the extra payment within 60 days from the date we notify you of your share of the deficiency, the amount will be charged as an indebtedness against the contract with interest compounded at the rate of 5% per year.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that 'Benefits will not be reduced or eliminated by any future amendments to our Articles of Incorporation or Bylaws.' The changed text removes this protection, implying that future amendments *could* reduce or eliminate benefits. This directly contradicts the 'MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY' section, which outlines how the society handles solvency issues, including potential benefit reductions. The original text ensured the Articles of Incorporation or Bylaws could not be used to reduce benefits, but the change removes this guarantee, creating a pathway for benefit reduction via amendments, which directly clashes with the solvency clause.

perturbation.original_text: ASSIGNMENT The ASSIGNMENT section is amended to include the following: You may transfer ownership of this contract in accordance with our bylaws. Society membership rights and privileges cannot be transferred or assigned.
perturbation.changed_text: ASSIGNMENT The ASSIGNMENT section is amended to include the following: You may transfer ownership of this contract in accordance with our bylaws.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the sentence 'Society membership rights and privileges cannot be transferred or assigned', the contract becomes ambiguous as to whether these rights and privileges can be transferred. This creates an internal contradiction because it now implies that the rights *could* be transferred creating uncertainity if the are trasferable or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: MEMBERSHIP. The person(s) named as the Insured(s) or the Annuitant(s) is a benefit member of the Society. Rights and privileges of membership are set forth in the Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws of the Society. These rights and privileges are separate from the ownership of this contract.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that society membership rights and privileges cannot be transferred or assigned. The changed text omits this statement, creating ambiguity and implying that these rights *could* be transferable. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text', which states that "Rights and privileges of membership are separate from the ownership of this contract.". Therefore, the omission creates a strong in-text contradiction, as per the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.
